title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text: AS92005 — Develop a digital technologies outcome (Level 1, Internal, 5 Credits)

title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text: AS92005 — Develop a digital technologies outcome (Level 1, Internal, 5 Credits)

title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text:
Phase 1: The Quarto Paradigm (Weeks 1–2)

Objective
Understand how Quarto differs from drag-and-drop or raw HTML workflows.


Topics

Installing and navigating RStudio or VS Code
Markdown basics (headings, lists, emphasis, links)
Static site generator concept (source files render to HTML)



Activity
Create a one-page About Me file and render it to HTML.

title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text:
Phase 2: Structuring the Website (Weeks 3–4)

Objective
Build a multi-page website using configuration files.


Topics

Creating a Quarto website project
Understanding _quarto.yml
Building navigation bars and sidebars
Applying a Bootswatch theme for styling



Activity
Create website skeleton with blank pages and working navigation.

title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text:
Phase 3: Integrating Data with R (Weeks 5–6)

Objective
Embed computational content in the website.


Topics

R code chunks in .qmd files
Loading environmental CSV data in R
Basic data checks and summary calculations
Creating one clear environmental chart



Activity
Choose either data/environment_nz.csv or data/waste_recycling_nz.csv, then add at least one working chart with a short interpretation.

title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text:
Phase 4: Development and Iteration (Weeks 7–8)

Objective
Develop full project content and improve quality.


Topics

Building complete page content
Functional testing (links, rendering, readability)
Peer testing for usability feedback and scientific clarity



Activity
Independent development with documented changes and evidence linked to Achieved/Merit/Excellence.

title: 10-Week Unit Plan
text:
Phase 5: Deployment and Submission (Weeks 9–10)

Objective
Publish and submit complete evidence.


Topics

Final render
Publish with Quarto Pub or GitHub Pages
Brief evaluation against intended outcome



Activity
Submit live URL, source files, and testing/evaluation evidence.

title: Teacher Guide
text:
Supports website outcomes with authentic development workflow.
Encourages computational thinking through R integration.
Produces clear evidence artifacts (source files, logs, rendered output).

title: Teacher Guide
text:
Supports website outcomes with authentic development workflow.
Encourages computational thinking through R integration.
Produces clear evidence artifacts (source files, logs, rendered output).

title: Teacher Guide
text:
Suggested assessment conditions

Individual project, milestone check-ins each fortnight.
Controlled class time plus managed homework expectations.
Evidence checkpoints: planning, draft build, testing log, final evaluation.

title: Teacher Guide
text:
Scaffolding recommendations

Provide starter _quarto.yml and sample .qmd page.
Use mini-lessons on common YAML and R errors.
Require short stand-up updates each week.
Provide the starter dataset in data/environment_nz.csv for initial success before students source their own data.
Model A/M/E writeups using the ame-evidence.qmd page.

title: Teacher Guide
text:
Authenticity checks

Ask students to explain one code chunk live.
Track iterative commits or dated development notes.
Compare early wireframe/plan to final output.

title: Assessment Project Brief (Student-Facing)
text:
Design and develop a multi-page website that communicates insights from an environmental dataset to a Year 11 audience.
Suggested inquiry focus: seasonal changes in rainfall, PM2.5 air quality, and river nitrate levels.
Alternative focus: waste and recycling trends (landfill tonnage, contamination, and household waste patterns).

title: Assessment Project Brief (Student-Facing)
text:
Design and develop a multi-page website that communicates insights from an environmental dataset to a Year 11 audience.
Suggested inquiry focus: seasonal changes in rainfall, PM2.5 air quality, and river nitrate levels.
Alternative focus: waste and recycling trends (landfill tonnage, contamination, and household waste patterns).

title: Assessment Project Brief (Student-Facing)
text:
Minimum requirements for Achieved

At least 4 pages with clear purpose.
Working navigation across all pages.
At least one R code chunk that loads and analyses environmental data.
Clear statement of which starter dataset you chose and why.
Evidence of testing and at least one improvement made from feedback.
Brief explanation of key design and technical choices.

title: Assessment Project Brief (Student-Facing)
text:
Merit / Excellence pathway
To move beyond Achieved, strengthen:

Fitness for purpose: content and layout closely match audience needs.
Development process: clear iterative improvements based on testing.
Quality of evidence: specific, justified decisions using data and feedback.
Evaluation depth: honest discussion of strengths, limitations, and next steps.

title: Assessment Project Brief (Student-Facing)
text:
Deliverables

Published site URL.
Quarto source files (.qmd, _quarto.yml, assets).
Testing log and evaluation write-up.
A short A/M/E evidence statement (use the A/M/E Evidence page as a model).

title: Assessment Project Brief (Student-Facing)
text:
Suggested timeline
Follow the 10-week plan page in this exemplar and adapt milestones to your class schedule.

title: AS92005 Website Exemplar
text:
This site is a complete example of how a Year 11 student can pass AS92005 using Quarto and R while still having a clear pathway to Merit/Excellence.
The project theme is environmental data in Aotearoa New Zealand (air quality, rainfall, and river nitrate trends).

title: AS92005 Website Exemplar
text:
This site is a complete example of how a Year 11 student can pass AS92005 using Quarto and R while still having a clear pathway to Merit/Excellence.
The project theme is environmental data in Aotearoa New Zealand (air quality, rainfall, and river nitrate trends).

title: AS92005 Website Exemplar
text:
What this project demonstrates

A multi-page digital outcome built for a specific purpose and audience.
Clear planning and staged development over one school term.
Functional website navigation and readable layout.
Integration of computational content using R code chunks.
Testing, iteration, and reflection evidence.

title: AS92005 Website Exemplar
text:
Environmental project context
Students investigate a local or national environmental question, for example:

How does rainfall change across the year?
Are PM2.5 air-quality readings higher in some months?
Is there a relationship between rainfall and river nitrate concentration?

This exemplar includes a starter dataset in data/environment_nz.csv.

title: AS92005 Website Exemplar
text:
Standard alignment (plain language)
To pass AS92005, students should show that they can:

Develop a digital outcome that works for users.
Use planning and development practices to build it.
Test and improve the outcome.
Explain choices and evaluate the final result.

Use this website as a model, then adapt topic and content to suit your own class context.

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
text: This page demonstrates the computational requirement of using R code within a Quarto website, using a starter environmental dataset.

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
text:
Step 1: Load the dataset

env <- read.csv("data/environment_nz.csv")
head(env)

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
text:
Step 2: Quick summary values

avg_pm25 <- mean(env$pm25)
total_rainfall <- sum(env$rainfall_mm)
avg_nitrate <- mean(env$river_nitrate_mgL)

data.frame(
  metric = c("Average PM2.5", "Total Rainfall (mm)", "Average River Nitrate (mg/L)"),
  value = c(round(avg_pm25, 2), round(total_rainfall, 1), round(avg_nitrate, 2))
)

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
text:
Step 3: Rainfall by month (bar chart)

barplot(
  env$rainfall_mm,
  names.arg = env$month,
  main = "Monthly Rainfall",
  xlab = "Month",
  ylab = "Rainfall (mm)",
  las = 2
)

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
section: Step 4: PM2.5 and river nitrate trend
text:
Step 4: PM2.5 and river nitrate trend

plot(
  env$month,
  env$pm25,
  type = "o",
  col = "steelblue",
  ylim = c(min(c(env$pm25, env$river_nitrate_mgL)), max(c(env$pm25, env$river_nitrate_mgL))),
  xlab = "Month",
  ylab = "Value",
  main = "PM2.5 and River Nitrate Across the Year"
)
lines(env$month, env$river_nitrate_mgL, type = "o", col = "darkgreen")
legend("topright", legend = c("PM2.5", "River Nitrate"), col = c("steelblue", "darkgreen"), lty = 1)

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
text:
Interpretation prompt
Write 2–3 sentences under each graph explaining:

what pattern the graph shows,
one possible reason for the trend,
one limitation of the dataset.

title: Environmental Data Demo (R Integration)
section: Option B demo: Waste and recycling dataset
text:
Option B demo: Waste and recycling dataset

waste <- read.csv("data/waste_recycling_nz.csv")
head(waste)


plot(
  waste$month,
  waste$landfill_tonnes,
  type = "o",
  col = "firebrick",
  ylim = c(min(c(waste$landfill_tonnes, waste$recycling_tonnes)), max(c(waste$landfill_tonnes, waste$recycling_tonnes))),
  xlab = "Month",
  ylab = "Tonnes",
  main = "Landfill vs Recycling Across the Year"
)
lines(waste$month, waste$recycling_tonnes, type = "o", col = "darkcyan")
legend("topright", legend = c("Landfill", "Recycling"), col = c("firebrick", "darkcyan"), lty = 1)

title: Achieved, Merit, Excellence Evidence Examples
text: Use this page as a model for writing evidence statements linked to your environmental website project.

title: Achieved, Merit, Excellence Evidence Examples
text:
Achieved example evidence

I created a 5-page Quarto site about local environmental trends.
My navigation links work across all pages.
I added R code chunks that load a CSV file and produce a chart.
I tested links and fixed one broken path to the data file.

title: Achieved, Merit, Excellence Evidence Examples
text:
Merit example evidence

I designed the site for Year 11 students and kept each page short with clear headings.
Peer feedback said my first graph was hard to read, so I changed colours, axis labels, and added a plain-English interpretation.
I ran weekly checks and improved layout consistency between pages.

title: Achieved, Merit, Excellence Evidence Examples
text:
Excellence example evidence

I justified design choices by linking them to user needs: concise text, high-contrast charts, and direct navigation for quick revision use.
I iteratively improved both technical and communication quality after testing, including better data explanations and clearer evidence of trends over time.
I evaluated limitations (small dataset, simulated values) and proposed next steps (real council/API data and interactive filtering).

title: Achieved, Merit, Excellence Evidence Examples
text:
Sentence starters students can adapt

Fitness for purpose: “I chose … because my audience needed …”
Iteration: “After testing with …, I changed … which improved …”
Evaluation depth: “A limitation of my outcome is …; next I would …”

title: Dataset Options
text: Students can choose one of two ready-to-use environmental datasets.

title: Dataset Options
section: Option A: Air + water + rainfall trends
text:
Option A: Air + water + rainfall trends

File: data/environment_nz.csv
Variables: month, PM2.5, rainfall, average temperature, river nitrate
Good inquiry questions:

Which months have the highest rainfall?
Does river nitrate change with seasonal rainfall?
Are PM2.5 values stable across the year?

title: Dataset Options
text:
Option B: Waste + recycling trends

File: data/waste_recycling_nz.csv
Variables: month, recycling tonnes, landfill tonnes, contamination %, household waste kg
Good inquiry questions:

Is landfill tonnage reducing over time?
How does contamination affect recycling quality?
Which months have highest household waste?

title: Dataset Options
text:
Quick start R code

# Choose one dataset path
# data_path <- "data/environment_nz.csv"
data_path <- "data/waste_recycling_nz.csv"

df <- read.csv(data_path)
head(df)

title: Dataset Options
text:
Teaching note
Start all students with a common dataset for the first chart, then allow topic differentiation after Week 6.

title: Evaluation Guide
text:
Address these prompts:

Who is the intended audience?
How does the website meet their needs?
Which design choices were most effective?
Which features did not work as well as expected?

title: Evaluation Guide
text:
Address these prompts:

Who is the intended audience?
How does the website meet their needs?
Which design choices were most effective?
Which features did not work as well as expected?

title: Evaluation Guide
text:
Evaluate your development process

What testing methods did you use? (self-test, peer test, checklist)
What specific feedback changed your outcome?
What evidence shows iteration over time?

title: Evaluation Guide
text:
Example reflective paragraph starter

My website was designed for Year 11 students learning about environmental trends. I used a simple navigation layout and short sections so users could quickly compare rainfall, PM2.5, and river nitrate results. Peer testing showed my first graph labels were unclear, so I updated axis titles, improved contrast, and added short interpretations under each graph. This improved usability because users could understand the data patterns without teacher help.

title: Evaluation Guide
text:
A/M/E evidence reminder
Use the A/M/E Evidence page to model how you write your final evidence statements.

text:

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text: Use this page before final submission to check your work against Achieved, Merit, and Excellence expectations.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
How to use

Tick the statements you can prove with evidence.
If you cannot prove it, improve your site or documentation.
Use this together with your development log, testing notes, and evaluation.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
1) Purpose and audience

Achieved

My website has a clear purpose.
I can name my intended audience.
My pages match the topic and purpose.



Merit

My content and layout are clearly designed for my audience.
My headings, labels, and explanations help users understand quickly.



Excellence

I can justify specific design decisions using audience needs.
My site communication is refined and consistently effective.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
2) Technical quality

Achieved

My navigation works across all pages.
All required pages render correctly.
My site has no major broken elements.



Merit

My site is consistent across pages (layout, style, readability).
My charts/tables are clear and labelled well.



Excellence

My site is polished and robust, with strong usability throughout.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
3) Environmental data and R integration

Achieved

I loaded an environmental dataset in R.
I produced at least one valid chart/table/value in my Quarto site.



Merit

My data output supports the key points I am making.
I explain what the chart/table shows in plain language.



Excellence

I use data strategically to communicate clear insights.
I justify why my data presentation choices are effective.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
4) Development process and testing

Achieved

I documented my process over time.
I tested my site and made at least one improvement.



Merit

I used peer or user feedback to make multiple improvements.
I can show before/after evidence for key changes.



Excellence

My iteration is systematic and evidence-led.
I can explain why each major change improved fitness for purpose.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
5) Evaluation quality

Achieved

I describe strengths and limitations of my final outcome.



Merit

I explain my choices with specific evidence from testing and data work.



Excellence

I critically evaluate limitations and propose realistic, high-value next steps.

title: Student Self-Assessment Checklist
text:
Final pre-submission check

Live site link works.
Source files are complete.
Development log is complete.
Testing evidence is included.
Evaluation is complete.
A/M/E evidence statement is complete.

title: Teacher Rubric Matrix (AS92005)
text: This rubric is a practical classroom guide for making consistent judgments against AS92005 using the environmental website context.

title: Teacher Rubric Matrix (AS92005)
text:
How to use this matrix

Make a holistic judgment using all available evidence.
Use this matrix with student source files, the live site, testing log, and evaluation.
If evidence sits between levels, record what is missing for the next grade boundary.

title: Teacher Rubric Matrix (AS92005)
text:
Achievement level indicators









Evidence area
Achieved
Merit
Excellence




Purpose and audience
Outcome has a clear purpose and intended audience.
Outcome is clearly shaped for audience needs with mostly effective communication.
Outcome is refined and highly fit for purpose, with deliberate choices that strongly support audience understanding.


Technical outcome quality
Multi-page Quarto site works; links and core features function.
Site is reliable, consistent, and mostly polished across pages/devices.
Site is robust, coherent, and polished with high usability and presentation quality.


Data integration (R)
Includes at least one valid R-generated output from environmental data.
Uses data outputs effectively to support key points and interpretation.
Uses data outputs strategically, with clear insight and justified interpretation quality.


Development process
Shows planning and basic testing with at least one improvement.
Shows iterative development with multiple improvements from feedback/testing.
Shows purposeful, ongoing iteration with evidence-driven refinements and strong process control.


Evaluation and justification
Describes what was done and some strengths/limitations.
Explains choices and improvements with specific examples.
Critically evaluates decisions, limitations, and next steps with clear justification linked to evidence.

title: Teacher Rubric Matrix (AS92005)
text:
Grade boundary guidance

To confirm Achieved

Core functionality is complete and usable.
Student demonstrates a valid development process and basic reflection.
R/data element is present and correctly integrated.



To confirm Merit

Improvements are clearly based on testing/feedback.
Design and communication decisions are mostly effective for the intended audience.
Evaluation is specific and supported by project evidence.



To confirm Excellence

Outcome is convincingly fit for purpose and well-refined.
Iteration is systematic, well-documented, and evidence-led.
Evaluation is critical, justified, and forward-looking.

title: Teacher Rubric Matrix (AS92005)
text:
Suggested evidence collection checklist

Live URL or locally rendered site view
Quarto source files (.qmd, _quarto.yml, dataset files)
Development log with dated changes
Testing evidence (self + peer)
Final evaluation statement
A/M/E evidence statement
